[University of New England](/wiki/University_of_New_England_(Australia) "University of New England (Australia)") [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")

(born 17 September 1979) is an Australian politician who has served as the 47th [premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") since 2023 and the leader of the [New South Wales Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party") since 2021. He has been the [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament#Australia "Member of parliament") (MP) for [Kogarah](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Kogarah "Electoral district of Kogarah") since 2015.

**Christopher John Minns** (born 17 September 1979) is an Australian politician who has served as the 47th [premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") since 2023 and the leader of the [New South Wales Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party") since 2021. He has been the [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_parliament#Australia "Member of parliament") (MP) for [Kogarah](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Kogarah "Electoral district of Kogarah") since 2015.

Prior to state politics, Minns served as the [deputy mayor of Hurstville](/wiki/City_of_Hurstville "City of Hurstville") from 2007 to 2008. He was first elected to the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly") at the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_New_South_Wales_state_election "2015 New South Wales state election"), and was [elected unopposed](/wiki/2021_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2021 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election") as leader of the Labor Party in the leadership election of June 2021, following the resignation of [Jodi McKay](/wiki/Jodi_McKay "Jodi McKay"). He led the party to victory at the [2023 election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election").

## Early and personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early and personal life")]

Christopher John Minns was born on 17 September 1979 in [St George, Sydney](/wiki/St_George,_Sydney "St George, Sydney"), the second of three children born to John Gerard Minns (1952–2024) and Cara Minns.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was raised in [Penshurst](/wiki/Penshurst,_New_South_Wales "Penshurst, New South Wales"),[[6]](#cite_note-abc-6) and attended [Marist College Kogarah](/wiki/Marist_College_Kogarah "Marist College Kogarah"),[[7]](#cite_note-NewGen-7) an independent Catholic secondary school in [Bexley](/wiki/Bexley,_New_South_Wales "Bexley, New South Wales"), a suburb of St George.

Minns attended the [University of New England](/wiki/University_of_New_England_(Australia) "University of New England (Australia)") in [Armidale](/wiki/Armidale "Armidale"), where he graduated with a [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts"). In 2012, he enrolled at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), United States, where he graduated with a [Master of Public Policy](/wiki/Master_of_Public_Policy "Master of Public Policy") in 2013.[[7]](#cite_note-NewGen-7)[[8]](#cite_note-NSW_Parl-8)

Minns is married and has three sons with his wife Anna. Upon his return from Princeton, while his wife pursued her own business opportunities Minns became the carer of his sons before nominating for parliament.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Minns is a supporter of [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") club the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs").[[10]](#cite_note-X-10)

Minns' father, John, died on 1 May 2024 after suffering a [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack"). In Minns' inaugural speech, he spoke about his love for his father, and thanked him for bringing Minns into the "Labor tribe".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Minns has a brother named Jim.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

Minns was first elected to office in 2004 as a Penshurst Ward councillor of the [Hurstville City Council](/wiki/Hurstville_City_Council "Hurstville City Council"), and was elected for a term as deputy mayor in 2007–2008; he left council at the 2008 election after serving a single term. He also worked on the staff of [Carl Scully](/wiki/Carl_Scully "Carl Scully") and [John Robertson](/wiki/John_Robertson_(New_South_Wales_politician) "John Robertson (New South Wales politician)").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Following [Luke Foley](/wiki/Luke_Foley "Luke Foley")'s resignation as NSW Labor leader and leader of the NSW opposition, Minns nominated for the roles. On 10 November 2018 Minns lost the [leadership spill](/wiki/2018_Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch)_leadership_election "2018 Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch) leadership election") to [Michael Daley](/wiki/Michael_Daley "Michael Daley"), 33 votes to 12.[[14]](#cite_note-14) After the [2019 state election](/wiki/2019_New_South_Wales_state_election "2019 New South Wales state election") Minns lost the [leadership election](/wiki/2019_Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch)_leadership_election "2019 Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch) leadership election") to [Jodi McKay](/wiki/Jodi_McKay "Jodi McKay") on a combined caucus and party membership vote of 60.5% to 39.5%.[[15]](#cite_note-15) He was appointed to the portfolios of transport and corrections in the [shadow cabinet](/wiki/Shadow_Ministry_of_Jodi_McKay "Shadow Ministry of Jodi McKay").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 2021 leadership challenge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2021 leadership challenge")]

Following Labor's defeat at the [Upper Hunter by-election](/wiki/2021_Upper_Hunter_state_by-election "2021 Upper Hunter state by-election") in May 2021 and a possible leadership challenge to McKay, a file titled *Why Chris Minns and Jamie Clements can never run the NSW Labor Party* was circulated from the office of deputy Labor leader [Yasmin Catley](/wiki/Yasmin_Catley "Yasmin Catley"). Minns was disappointed with the lack of explanation or communication from McKay and Catley over the file circulation, and resigned from shadow cabinet on 26 May.[[17]](#cite_note-17) He was the second MP to resign from shadow cabinet in two days after shadow treasurer [Walt Secord](/wiki/Walt_Secord "Walt Secord"), a close supporter of Minns.[[18]](#cite_note-18) On 31 May 2021, after McKay resigned as party leader, Minns announced he would run for party leadership.[[19]](#cite_note-19) If [Michael Daley](/wiki/Michael_Daley "Michael Daley") and Minns had contested for party leadership, it would have been Minns's third [leadership contest](/wiki/2021_Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch)_leadership_election "2021 Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch) leadership election") and his second one versus Daley. Minns’s leadership bid was publicly supported by more than a dozen senior Labor MPs including Penny Sharpe, Ryan Park, Jihad Dib, and Prue Car.[[20]](#cite_note-20) On 4 June 2021, Daley pulled out of the leadership contest, allowing Minns to be elected to the position of leader unopposed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Opposition leader

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Opposition leader")]

See also: [Shadow Ministry of Chris Minns](/wiki/Shadow_Ministry_of_Chris_Minns "Shadow Ministry of Chris Minns")

In the 2023 NSW election campaign, Minns made election promises to invest further into public services.[[22]](#cite_note-22) He had been criticised for being reluctant to promise reform on [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") in gambling,[[23]](#cite_note-23) however on 16 January Minns released a plan to reform gambling, which would ban donations from clubs (gambling organisations) to political parties and promises a cashless gaming card trial, which would last for 12 months and cover 500 of the approximately 86,480 (0.58%) [pokies](/wiki/Slot_machine "Slot machine") machines (slots).[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

#### 2023 election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2023 election")]

See also: [Minns ministry](/wiki/Minns_ministry "Minns ministry")

Minns led the Labor Party to victory at the [2023 New South Wales state election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election") on 25 March, defeating the incumbent [Liberal–National Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(Australia) "Coalition (Australia)"), returning Labor to power at the state level for the first time since 2011.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Despite winning the election, Labor did not win enough seats to govern in [majority](/wiki/Majority_government "Majority government"), but were able to form government with the support of three [independents](/wiki/Independent_politicians "Independent politicians") [Alex Greenwich](/wiki/Alex_Greenwich "Alex Greenwich"), [Greg Piper](/wiki/Greg_Piper "Greg Piper") and [Joe McGirr](/wiki/Joe_McGirr "Joe McGirr").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Premier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Premier")]

In 2024, Minns supported the [federal Labor government](/wiki/Albanese_government "Albanese government")'s [age verification system](/wiki/Online_Safety_Amendment "Online Safety Amendment") that would bar users under the age of 16 from using certain forms of social media.[[28]](#cite_note-1_News_social_media_ban-28)

Between May and July 2025, Minns's government amended tenancy legislation, first increasing protections for renters then partially rolling the changes back. In May, amendments to the *Residential Tenancies Act 2010* came into force. Described by the government as "ending no grounds evictions", the most publicised of the changes was a provision requiring landlords to provide a compliant reason for evictions, along with a piece of supporting evidence.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29) The Tenants' Union of NSW described the changes as "a significant improvement for NSW renters".[[30]](#cite_note-30) Other changes included restricting the authority of landlords to restrict pets, prohibiting rent increases within one year of the previous increase, and prohibiting fees for background checks.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-29)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

After five weeks, the evidence requirement was abolished by an amendment to the Residential Tenancies Regulation 2019, rendering it sufficient for landlords to merely submit a written statement. According to the Tenants' Union, the government "quietly removed key safeguards".[[31]](#cite_note-31) Dr Chris Martin of the UNSW City Futures Research Centre characterised this as the introduction of a "renovictions" loophole.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 2024, the government announced a 3% per annum pay rise for teachers in public schools over three years. The Minister for Industrial Relations said that it "reaffirms the Minns Labor Government’s new industrial relations framework is working".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The culling of [brumbies](/wiki/Brumby_shooting "Brumby shooting") (wild, introduced horses) in [Kosciuszko National Park](/wiki/Kosciuszko_National_Park "Kosciuszko National Park") was implemented to reduce their numbers and protect native flora and fauna.[[34]](#cite_note-34) A bill passed parliament on 27 November 2025 to repeal the *[Kosciuszko Wild Horse Heritage Act 2018](/wiki/Kosciuszko_Wild_Horse_Heritage_Act_2018 "Kosciuszko Wild Horse Heritage Act 2018")*.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Minns's government passed a law in early 2025 which restricted protests near places of worship.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The [NSW Council for Civil Liberties](/wiki/NSW_Council_for_Civil_Liberties "NSW Council for Civil Liberties") and legal scholars criticised this action as authoritarian and undermining freedom of assembly.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Opposition legislators, such as [Sue Higginson](/wiki/Sue_Higginson "Sue Higginson") of the [Greens](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Greens "New South Wales Greens"), raised concerns over [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) On 19 June 2025, a legal challenge was brought before the [Supreme Court of New South Wales](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Supreme Court of New South Wales") against the legislation.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) The judge overseeing the case, Justice Anna Mitchelmore [SC](/wiki/Senior_Counsel "Senior Counsel"), ruled on 16 October that the law was unconstitutional, due to its impermissible burden on the  [Australian Constitution's](/wiki/Australian_Constitution "Australian Constitution") [implied freedom of political communication](/wiki/Implied_freedom_of_political_communication "Implied freedom of political communication").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Concerns from opposition legislators about the circumstances surrounding the passage of the places of worship law (and other legislation from the same time-frame), have led to the establishment of a parliamentary inquiry.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In August 2025, Minns's government announced that they will complete [Woollahra station](/wiki/Woollahra_railway_station "Woollahra railway station") in order to enable rezoning for additional housing development in the area. Opposition Leader and Liberal Party member [Kellie Sloane](/wiki/Kellie_Sloane "Kellie Sloane") [opposed](/wiki/Nimby "Nimby") the upgrade of the station.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In September 2025, Minns's government signalled its intention to significantly amend the *[Environmental Planning and Assessment Act 1979](/wiki/Environmental_Planning_and_Assessment_Act_1979 "Environmental Planning and Assessment Act 1979")*, with the legislation now including the government's existing [Housing Delivery Authority](/wiki/Housing_Delivery_Authority "Housing Delivery Authority") and a new Development Coordination Authority.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In October 2025, Minns announced that the government would take back control of [Northern Beaches Hospital](/wiki/Northern_Beaches_Hospital "Northern Beaches Hospital") from [Healthscope](/wiki/Healthscope "Healthscope"), its private operator, at a cost of $190 million. Controversy had arisen surrounding the hospital following the death of a two-year-old child at the hospital. Legislation has banned future [public-private partnerships](/wiki/Public-private_partnerships "Public-private partnerships") in the hospital system.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Following the [2025 Bondi Beach shooting](/wiki/2025_Bondi_Beach_shooting "2025 Bondi Beach shooting") on 15 December 2025, Minns announced that the New South Wales Parliament would reconvene on 22 December to introduce new firearms laws and a ban on protests following a "terror designation."[[51]](#cite_note-51) On 24 December, the NSW Parliament passed an omnibus bill, which tightened access to firearms and restricted mass protests following terrorism incidents.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The bill passed despite some controversies, with the [Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party](/wiki/Shooters,_Fishers_and_Farmers_Party "Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party") and [the Nationals](/wiki/National_Party_of_Australia "National Party of Australia") opposing the gun-law reforms. [The Greens](/wiki/Australian_Greens "Australian Greens") and some Labor members voiced concerns about alleged [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") in the restrictions on [freedom of assembly](/wiki/Freedom_of_assembly "Freedom of assembly").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

His government's response to a protest against the visit of Israeli president [Isaac Herzog](/wiki/Isaac_Herzog "Isaac Herzog") triggered allegations of [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality") and [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding").[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) Following sustained public pressure, the [Law Enforcement Conduct Commission](/wiki/Law_Enforcement_Conduct_Commission "Law Enforcement Conduct Commission") announced an investigation into police actions on 13 February.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Minns has hinted that he intends to retire from politics, prior to the 2031 election.[[63]](#cite_note-Retire2032-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) However, he has stated his plan to contest the [2027 state election](/wiki/2027_New_South_Wales_state_election "2027 New South Wales state election") as party leader.[[63]](#cite_note-Retire2032-63)

## Political views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Political views")]

Minns is a member of [Labor Right](/wiki/Labor_Right "Labor Right"),[[65]](#cite_note-65) and was assistant secretary of the NSW Labor Party. He has been described as a [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In 2003, while adviser to minister for roads and housing [Carl Scully](/wiki/Carl_Scully "Carl Scully"), Minns attended the Australia Israel Jewish Affairs Council ([AIJAC](/wiki/AIJAC "AIJAC")) inaugural [Rambam](/wiki/Rambam "Rambam") trip to Israel. Attending alongside Minns were [Scott Morrison](/wiki/Scott_Morrison "Scott Morrison"), [Bill Shorten](/wiki/Bill_Shorten "Bill Shorten") and foreign policy adviser to federal opposition leader [Simon Crean](/wiki/Simon_Crean "Simon Crean") Carl Ungerer.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In his inaugural speech in the Legislative Assembly, Minns criticised the state government's sale of NSW's electricity assets and called for mandatory [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") lessons in New South Wales schools.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 2019, Minns argued in favour for the [legalisation of cannabis](/wiki/Legalisation_of_cannabis "Legalisation of cannabis") during a party meeting but has since walked back this position since becoming premier.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Minns opposed legislation that would enable [voluntary euthanasia](/wiki/Voluntary_euthanasia "Voluntary euthanasia").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Views on union influence in the Labor Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Minns&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Views on union influence in the Labor Party")]

Additionally, Minns called for a reduction in union influence in the Labor Party in favour of "increasing representation of ordinary members of our party who have more diverse voices", stating that while trade unions were integral to the success and heritage of the Labor Party, the party also needs to represent those who are not in a trade union, and that will mean taking steps to reduce union control on Labor's conference floor.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

[Bob Nanva](/wiki/Bob_Nanva "Bob Nanva"), national secretary of the [Rail, Tram and Bus Union](/wiki/Australian_Rail_Tram_and_Bus_Industry_Union "Australian Rail Tram and Bus Industry Union"), while acknowledging that Minns had been "an extraordinarily effective Assistant General Secretary of the ALP", rebuked him for being "seriously mistaken" on his views about unions.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Additionally, both [Mark Buttigieg](/wiki/Mark_Buttigieg "Mark Buttigieg") and NSW Labor Party secretary Jamie Clements disagreed with Minns's contention regarding unions.[[73]](#cite_note-73) By 2019, Minns reportedly no longer held those views, according to HSU NSW state secretary Gerard Hayes.[[74]](#cite_note-74)
